---
title: "How Fast Does New York Fix It?"
subtitle: "Sixteen years of 311 data, one map, and a few surprises"
author: "Jacob Patterson-Stein"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    center: false
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    embed-resources: true
---

## {background-color="#1a535c"}

::: {.kicker style="color:#4ecdc4;"}
NYC 311 · 2010–2026
:::

# How Fast Does New York Fix It?

Sixteen years of 311 data, one map, and a few surprises.

Jacob Patterson-Stein · Data & Product Analysis

## A Personal Prior {.dark-slide=false}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](images/snow-pile.jpeg)
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
::: {.kicker}
A Personal Prior
:::

During the 2025 blizzard, a gas station near my apartment dumped its whole lot's snow on the corner by the crosswalk.

Kids, dogs, everyone near my school block had to walk into a busy avenue to get around it. After a close call with a car, I called 311 — expecting maybe two weeks, if the snow didn't melt first.

**The next day, a path was cut clean through the pile.**

So what was the actual probability my request got answered in a day — and why were my priors so wrong?
:::
:::

## The Question

::: {.kicker}
The Question
:::

Every New Yorker has filed a 311 complaint — a busted radiator in January, a car blocking the driveway, a streetlight out for weeks.

**And every one has wondered the same thing: is anyone actually going to fix this, and when?**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**16 years**\
of public 311 data, 2010–2026
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Tens of millions**\
of requests, cleaned and unified
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**1 question**\
what are the odds it gets fixed?
:::
:::

## The Instrument {background-color="#1a535c" .dark-slide}

::: {.kicker}
The Instrument
:::

### A block-level tool for measuring city performance.

For any census tract and any complaint type, the map estimates the probability a request resolves within 3 hours, a day, a week, or a month — built to support oversight, not just curiosity.

The history behind it — sixteen years, from Bloomberg to the current Mamdani administration — is where the policy findings live.

[View the live map →](https://jacobpstein.github.io/311_probs/)

## The Hard Part

::: {.kicker}
The Hard Part
:::

### It isn't the map. It's the small blocks.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout}
**Busy block**\
1,800 of 2,000 closed same-day\
**90%** — a solid estimate.
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout}
**Quiet block**\
2 of 3 closed same-day\
**67%** — a coin flip dressed up as a statistic.
:::
:::
:::

Show that on a map and you've drawn a lie in bright colors.

## The Fix

::: {.kicker}
The Fix
:::

### Let the small blocks borrow from their neighborhood.

A tract with thousands of requests speaks for itself. A tract with three leans on its neighborhood, then its borough.

The less local data a block has, the more it defers — and the map shows its work: every estimate carries a data-strength indicator.

## The Audit {background-color="#1a535c" .dark-slide}

::: {.kicker}
Stress-Testing The Model
:::

### I audited my own model. It failed twice.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Bug 1 — Under-pooling**\
The pooling routine quietly stopped short of the true optimum. Fixed with a method that finds the exact answer.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Bug 2 — Overconfident bands**\
An even/odd-day split test showed "90%" intervals covering reality only about half the time. Added measured month-to-month drift.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Idea killed on purpose**\
Blending in last year's same-season data tested as a statistical dead heat — so it didn't ship.
:::
:::

**51,150 published estimates, re-checked one by one.**

## Finding 1

::: {.kicker}
Finding 1
:::

### The city looks faster. Mostly because of what we count.

Hold the mix of complaint types fixed, and most of the headline gain from Bloomberg to Adams disappears. The bulk of the "improvement" is composition, not faster service.

The comparison stops at Adams on purpose: the current Mamdani administration, in office since January 2026, has only five months of matured data — too provisional for a verdict.

## Finding 1, continued {background-color="#1a535c" .dark-slide}

::: {.kicker}
Finding 1, continued
:::

### Noise complaints are eating the average.

The 311 app made noise complaints trivial to file. NYPD closes them almost instantly — an officer swings by, the party's over, ticket closed.

**Stack the deck with fast complaints and the average looks great — without a single agency getting better at its job.**

## Finding 2

::: {.kicker}
Finding 2
:::

### The real story is agency-shaped, not administration-shaped.

- **HPD** (heat, plumbing, electrical, paint) — steady climb, every administration
- **Infrastructure** (water system, traffic signals) — slipping, across the board
- **Police-handled** (parking, noise) — pinned near-ceiling for 16 straight years

None of these trajectories start or stop at an inauguration.

## Finding 3 {background-color="#1a535c" .dark-slide}

::: {.kicker}
Finding 3
:::

### COVID wasn't a slowdown. It was a composition shock.

Locked-down New Yorkers filed a flood of noise complaints — and those close fast, so the citywide average actually rose.

Underneath, services split: heat kept improving while derelict-vehicle and traffic-signal work slowed.

**The COVID window is quarantined from any administration's record — averaging it in would libel whoever was in office.**

## Reading The Map Honestly

::: {.kicker}
Reading The Map Honestly
:::

### Two shading modes, so the colors never fool you.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Absolute**\
Fixed 0–100% scale — colors mean the same thing everywhere.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Relative**\
Stretched to the on-screen range — reveals variation, but the legend always prints the true spread.
:::
:::

## Lessons {background-color="#1a535c" .dark-slide}

::: {.kicker}
What We'd Tell The Next Team
:::

### Three lessons from sixteen years of city data.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**The data-cleaning is the science.**\
Negative durations, duplicate submissions, bulk-closure purges — how you handle these is the analysis, not preamble to it.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**"Recorded" is not "real."**\
A resolved ticket means the city closed it — not always that the problem got fixed.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Let the data kill your ideas.**\
Seasonal blending, confident intervals, tidy narratives — the data vetoed all three, and the work is better for it.
:::
:::

## Closing

### Point at your block. Pick your problem. See the odds.

- A proper seasonal model, now that there are sixteen winters to learn from.
- Neighborhood equity questions — same complaint, same agency, different places.
- A public era-comparison tool, so anyone can pull up their own block's history.

[View the live map →](https://jacobpstein.github.io/311_probs/) · [github.com/jacobpstein](https://github.com/jacobpstein)

## Technical Annex I — A Bayesian Primer {background-color="#1a535c" .dark-slide}

::: {.kicker}
Technical Annex I
:::

### The framework: why Bayesian, not just a raw proportion.

A frequentist estimate for a tract is just its raw proportion: requests closed same-day, divided by requests filed. With three requests, that number can only ever be 0%, 33%, 67%, or 100%.

Bayes' rule instead treats the tract's true rate as uncertain, gives it a **prior** informed by similar tracts, updates it with the tract's own data — its **likelihood** — to get a **posterior** estimate.

**Crucially, the prior isn't guessed in advance — it's fit from the city's own data, tract by tract.**

## Technical Annex II — Model Structure

::: {.kicker}
Technical Annex II
:::

### The model: a four-level Bayesian hierarchy.

Each tract's outcomes are modeled as draws over the nine time-to-resolution bins — a Dirichlet–Multinomial, with the within-a-day slice shown here for clarity — governed by an unknown resolution rate θ~tract~. That rate is pulled toward its neighborhood's rate, which is pulled toward its borough's, and finally toward the citywide rate.

$$w_i = \frac{n_i}{n_i + \kappa} \qquad \hat\theta_i = w_i \cdot \hat\theta_{tract,i} + (1-w_i) \cdot \hat\theta_{nbhd}$$

n~i~ = requests observed in tract i · κ = fitted pooling strength · the same structure recurses up the chain: tract → neighborhood → borough → citywide root.

Every published estimate ships with its own **data-strength indicator** — how much of the estimate is the tract's own record versus a borrowed neighborhood estimate.

## Technical Annex III — Validation {background-color="#1a535c" .dark-slide}

::: {.kicker}
Technical Annex III
:::

### Validation: what the audit actually found and fixed.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**κ estimation**\
Minka's fixed-point update converged well short of the true optimum. Replaced with direct optimization of the marginal likelihood over κ.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Interval coverage**\
An even/odd-day split test showed nominal 90% intervals covering reality only ~50% of the time. Added a measured **regime variance** term for month-to-month drift.
:::
:::

All 51,150 published tract × complaint-type estimates were re-derived under the corrected model before publishing.
